title: Working with Tables in Word
description: Create, format, and manage tables in Word for schedules, reports, and structured data presentation.
objectives: Add borders and shading to tables
Use simple formulas in table cells
Create a class schedule or meeting timetable
content:
Why Tables Matter in Word

Tables are not just for Excel. In Word, tables help you present structured information like meeting schedules, staff directories, student registrations, and product catalogs. A well-formatted table makes information easy to scan and understand.

Creating a Table

Go to Insert > Table and choose the number of rows and columns. You can also draw a table manually or insert a pre-formatted table from Quick Tables.

Merging and Splitting Cells

To merge cells (combine two or more cells into one): select the cells, right-click, and choose Merge Cells. This is useful for creating a title row that spans all columns. To split a cell into multiple cells: right-click the cell and choose Split Cells.

Sorting Table Data

Word can sort table data alphabetically or numerically. Select the table, go to Layout > Sort, choose the column to sort by, and select Ascending or Descending. This is useful for sorting names, dates, or scores.

Formulas in Word Tables

Word tables can use simple formulas. Click in a cell, go to Layout > Formula, and type a formula like =SUM(ABOVE) or =AVERAGE(LEFT). This is handy for simple calculations in a report or invoice.

Workplace Scenario

Your manager asks you to create a meeting timetable for the next board meeting. You insert a 7-row, 3-column table. You merge the top row and type 'Board Meeting Schedule' as the title. You fill in the days and times, sort the entries by time, and apply a professional border style. The table is inserted into a memo and sent to all staff.

Example Table: Meeting Schedule

TimeTopicOwner
09:00 - 09:30Welcome and IntroductionChairperson
09:30 - 10:30Budget ReviewFinance Manager
10:30 - 10:45Tea Break-
10:45 - 12:00Project UpdatesProject Lead

downloads.title: Meeting Schedule Template
downloads.description: A Word table template for meeting schedules

exercise: Create a table in Word with 5 rows and 4 columns. Add headers: Name, Department, Phone, Email. Fill in 4 rows of fictional staff data. Merge the top row and add a title 'Staff Directory'. Apply a border style and shading to the header row. Sort the table by Department in alphabetical order. Save as Staff_Directory.docx.

quiz.question: How do you merge two cells in a Word table?
options: Select the cells, right-click, and choose Merge Cells
quiz.explanation: Select the cells you want to merge, right-click, and choose Merge Cells from the menu.

quiz.question: Where do you find the Sort option for a table in Word?
quiz.explanation: The Sort option is found in the Table Layout (or Layout) tab when you have a table selected.

quiz.question: Which formula would you use to add all numbers in the cells above?
quiz.explanation: =SUM(ABOVE) adds all numbers in the cells directly above the current cell in a Word table.

quiz.question: Why would you merge cells in a table?
options: To create a title row that spans all columns
quiz.explanation: Merging cells is commonly used to create a single title cell that spans across all columns at the top of a table.
